topicArea: Debt, the Open Ledger & Which Balances to Keep

questions.passage:
The bill arrives and one friend takes it with a gesture that forbids argument. The other submits, performs gratitude, and says the sentence the evening was always going to produce: I owe you one. Neither will write anything down, both will remember, and the unwritten entry will sit open between them, comfortably, for months. Two friends who would insist they keep no accounts have just made an entry in a ledger older than money, and the entry's openness, it turns out, is the point.

Obligation is a social technology that long precedes coinage. Favors, feasts, and gifts of labor bound households and villages in webs of owing, and in many recorded societies the debts were engineered never to close: repayment was made deliberately inexact, a little more or a little less, so that some balance always remained and with it a reason to return. To be indebted was to be connected; the person who owed you would come back. Full and exact settlement had a precise social meaning, and it was not virtue. It was farewell, the accounts cleared because the relationship was over.

Money rewrote the instrument. Quantification made debt exact, and exactness made it transferable and enforceable: an obligation with a number on it can be recorded, assigned, sold to a stranger, and collected by a bearer who never met the borrower. The gains were enormous, since numbers let strangers transact at scale, credit could travel farther than trust ever could on foot, and the written ledger outlived, and outperformed, the fallible memory it replaced. But the same operation stripped the obligation of its relationship. A debt that anyone can hold is a debt that binds no one in particular, and the bond became a balance.

The moral residue is everywhere in the language, which still cannot discuss right conduct without bookkeeping: we owe explanations, redeem ourselves, forgive trespasses, pay debts to society. The vocabulary carries an old double standard, the debtor bearing a moral weight the creditor somehow escapes, as if owing were a character flaw and being owed a form of cleanliness. History has been less settled than the axiom debts must be paid suggests: the same traditions that ranked usury among the gravest sins also built jubilees and amnesties, scheduled forgiveness that wiped the tablets clean, and modern bankruptcy descends from that counter-tradition, the recognition that a society can hold the axiom too tightly for its own good.

Who owes and who is owed has never been a neutral fact. Lending tracks power in one direction and dependence in the other, and the indebted are, notoriously, governable: the worker with payments due thinks twice about the strike, the graduate with a decade of installments ahead chooses the safe job, the indebted state accepts terms no sovereign would otherwise sign. Yet the dependence runs both ways at scale, as the old joke insists, owe the bank a thousand and the bank owns you, owe it a million and you own the bank, since the creditor's fortunes are hostage to the debtor's survival.

None of this licenses the romantic conclusion that debt is a trap and cash the only clean relation. Credit is also the technology by which people without fortunes act: the mortgage, the harvest loan, the borrowed stake that starts the business. A world without borrowing is a world where only the already-rich do anything, which is why the serious questions are never debt versus no debt but terms, exits, and the distribution of downside, who pays when the harvest fails, and whether the system has doors or only walls. The jubilee was not sentimentality; it was an engineered exit, relief designed into the architecture.

Which returns the matter to the dinner table. I owe you one is not bookkeeping; it is bonding, the deliberately unpaid balance that keeps the account, and therefore the friendship, open. Formal debt closed its ledgers with numbers and gained the whole world of strangers, a real gain paid for in a real currency. A culture fluent in both should keep the distinction straight: some debts exist to be settled, and some exist so that they never quite are, and the wisdom is in knowing which ledger one is holding.
questions.stem: Which of the following best expresses the main idea of the passage?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Because quantified debt strips every obligation of its relationship, informal favor economies are morally superior to formal credit systems in every respect.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The axiom that debts must always be paid has proven to be history's single most destructive moral principle, as the jubilee traditions amply demonstrate.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Debt is an old bonding technology that money made exact, transferable, and impersonal; the wisdom lies in knowing which balances to settle and which to keep open.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The mutual dependence of creditor and debtor shows that the power asymmetries attributed to debt are largely illusory.

explanations.A: This choice converts a traced loss into a ranking the passage refuses. The bond became a balance names what quantification stripped, but the gains are called enormous, strangers transacting at scale, and the sixth paragraph expressly blocks the romantic conclusion that cash is the only clean relation.
explanations.B: This choice inflates the fourth paragraph's counter-tradition into a verdict on the axiom. Jubilees and bankruptcy show a society can hold the axiom too tightly, a corrective within a history described as unsettled, not a declaration that the principle itself is history's most destructive.
explanations.C: Correct. The passage traces obligation from the engineered-open balances that bound pre-monetary societies, through money's rewriting, exact, transferable, binding no one in particular, past the moral residue and the power asymmetries, to the closing distinction: some debts exist to be settled and some so that they never quite are, and the wisdom is knowing which ledger one holds.
explanations.D: This choice stretches the old joke over the whole fifth paragraph. The million-dollar reversal qualifies the asymmetry at scale; the paragraph's core stands, the indebted are governable, the worker, the graduate, the state, and mutual hostage-taking at the top does not dissolve discipline below.

questions.stem: The primary function of the sixth paragraph is to:

choices.label: A
choices.text: concede that the moral double standard of the fourth paragraph applies only to consumer borrowing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: block the anti-debt conclusion by crediting what borrowing enables, then relocate the questions to terms, exits, and downside.

choices.label: C
choices.text: argue that mortgage lending and harvest lending should both be exempt from the interest restrictions that properly govern other consumer credit.

choices.label: D
choices.text: demonstrate that the celebrated jubilee traditions ultimately failed because their designers were economically naive.

explanations.A: This choice invents a scope restriction on a different paragraph's point. The double standard, debtor's burden, creditor's cleanliness, is a feature of moral vocabulary generally; the sixth paragraph neither mentions it nor confines it to consumer credit.
explanations.B: Correct. The paragraph opens by refusing the romantic conclusion, debt as trap, cash as the only clean relation, credits credit as the technology by which people without fortunes act, mortgage, harvest loan, borrowed stake, warns that a world without borrowing reserves action for the already-rich, and then names the real questions: terms, exits, who bears the downside, doors rather than walls.
explanations.C: This choice converts examples into a regulatory carve-out. The mortgage and harvest loan illustrate what credit enables; no interest policy is proposed for them or anything else.
explanations.D: This choice reverses the paragraph's final sentence. The jubilee was not sentimentality; it was an engineered exit, relief designed into the architecture, praise for the design, not a diagnosis of naivety or failure.

questions.stem: The claim that full and exact settlement was farewell, in the second paragraph, most nearly means that:

choices.label: A
choices.text: in economies of engineered obligation, clearing a balance completely signaled the relationship's end.

choices.label: B
choices.text: pre-monetary societies lacked the arithmetic to compute exact repayments.

choices.label: C
choices.text: departing villagers were customarily required to settle all of their outstanding accounts fully before leaving the community.

choices.label: D
choices.text: creditors mourned repayment because it ended their income from interest.

explanations.A: Correct. The paragraph builds the logic explicitly: repayment was made deliberately inexact so that some balance always remained and with it a reason to return, and to be indebted was to be connected. Exact settlement therefore carried a precise social meaning, the accounts cleared because the relationship was over, closure of the ledger as closure of the bond, which the final paragraph replays at the dinner table.
explanations.B: This choice mistakes design for incapacity. The inexactness was deliberate, a little more or a little less by intention; societies that engineered imprecision could evidently compute precision, and chose against it.
explanations.C: This choice reverses the causal direction. Settlement did not precede departure as a rule; settlement meant departure, the farewell enacted in the clearing, and no exit requirement appears anywhere in the paragraph.
explanations.D: This choice imports interest income into an economy of favors and feasts. The creditor's loss at settlement was the connection, the person who owed you would come back, not a revenue stream; interest belongs to the monetized instrument of the next paragraph.

questions.stem: The author's attitude toward the axiom that debts must be paid is best described as:

choices.label: A
choices.text: endorsing, since the passage treats reliable settlement as the essential foundation of commercial trust between total strangers.

choices.label: B
choices.text: dismissive, treating the axiom as nothing more than a creditor's self-serving slogan with no legitimate social function.

choices.label: C
choices.text: agnostic, declining to discuss the axiom's history or its exceptions.

choices.label: D
choices.text: historicizing, presenting the axiom as less settled than it sounds, held alongside usury prohibitions, jubilees, and bankruptcy.

explanations.A: This choice mistakes the third paragraph's gains for a moral endorsement. Enforceability let strangers transact, a credited achievement, but the fourth paragraph immediately unsettles the axiom with the counter-tradition, and the sixth relocates the serious questions away from payment's sanctity entirely.
explanations.B: This choice overshoots the correction into contempt. The axiom is shown to be incompletely honored, not illegitimate; the passage's own account of credit's enablements presupposes that repayment normally matters, and the critique targets holding the axiom too tightly.
explanations.C: This choice describes the opposite of the fourth paragraph's method, which is precisely to give the axiom a history: sins of usury, scheduled amnesties, tablets wiped clean, bankruptcy as descendant.
explanations.D: Correct. The paragraph frames the axiom as sounding more settled than history was: the same traditions that ranked usury among the gravest sins also built jubilees and amnesties, and modern bankruptcy descends from that counter-tradition, the recognition that a society can hold the axiom too tightly for its own good. The attitude is neither worship nor dismissal but placement in time.

questions.stem: After a shared taxi, one friend immediately sends the other an electronic payment request for $4.85, itemized to the cent. Based on the second, third, and final paragraphs, the author would most likely observe that the request:

choices.label: A
choices.text: honors the friendship by promptly preventing any quiet resentment that an unpaid balance might otherwise breed between them.

choices.label: B
choices.text: demonstrates the double standard, since the requester takes on the creditor's cleanliness.

choices.label: C
choices.text: proves conclusively that the friends' relationship was already fundamentally commercial rather than genuinely personal in nature.

choices.label: D
choices.text: applies the stranger's instrument to a friend's ledger, closing with exactness a balance whose openness could do the bonding.

explanations.A: This choice supplies a resentment theory the passage never offers. In its account the open balance is comfortable, sitting between the dinner friends for months as connection; prevention of grievance is not among exactness's listed virtues, and farewell is its social meaning.
explanations.B: This choice misapplies the fourth paragraph's asymmetry. The double standard concerns moral weight, debtor burdened, creditor clean, in the culture's vocabulary; a four-dollar request is not an exhibit of that hierarchy, and the scenario's engine is exactness, not standing.
explanations.C: This choice reads the instrument as proof about the relationship. The passage's point runs the other way: the tools carry meanings, and applying the exact one is a choice with social content, not a revelation that the friendship was commerce all along.
explanations.D: Correct. Quantification made debt exact and impersonal, the stranger's gain; among intimates, the second paragraph's engineering ran opposite, repayment deliberately inexact so a balance and a reason to return remained. Settling $4.85 to the cent closes a ledger the final paragraph says friendship keeps open on purpose, exactness deployed where the unpaid remainder was the bond.

questions.stem: Which of the following, if true, would most weaken the second paragraph's claim that deliberately open balances sustained social connection?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that some pre-monetary societies also used standardized units of grain or cattle to measure their largest obligations.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Evidence that feasts and gifts of labor were often given competitively, primarily to display wealth and status before rivals.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Ethnographies finding prompt, exact repayment the norm among the closest kin, with relationships enduring identically either way.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that written records of obligations existed centuries before coined money.

explanations.A: This choice shows measurement coexisting with the favor economy, which the claim survives easily; standardized units for large obligations do not touch whether everyday balances were held open or what the openness did.
explanations.B: This choice adds a motive, display, to practices the claim already includes. Competitive feasting still generates webs of owing, and status-seeking givers create precisely the open balances the mechanism requires; the why does not disturb the what.
explanations.C: Correct. The claim's mechanism is that inexact repayment left a remainder, and the remainder kept people returning, connection riding on openness. Exact and prompt settlement as the norm among the closest relations, with closeness unaffected by whether balances stayed open, would show the bond flourishing without the remainder, the engineering unnecessary and the openness idle.
explanations.D: This choice concerns record-keeping's antiquity, which bears on the third paragraph's ledger-versus-memory transition, not on whether open balances bonded; written obligations can be engineered open as easily as remembered ones.

questions.stem: Which of the following findings would most strengthen the fifth paragraph's claim that the indebted are governable?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Evidence that consumer borrowing rises during economic expansions.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Studies finding indebted workers less likely to strike, change jobs, or report violations, the caution scaling with balances and fading after payoff.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Surveys finding that most borrowers describe their loans as freely chosen.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Evidence that heavily indebted sovereign states receive considerably more frequent official visits from their major creditor delegations each fiscal year.

explanations.A: This choice tracks credit's cyclical volume, which says nothing about conduct under obligation; more borrowing in good times is compatible with debtors who are bold, timid, or anything between.
explanations.B: Correct. The claim is behavioral, the worker with payments due thinks twice about the strike, and this finding measures exactly that: strike reluctance, job immobility, silence about violations. The dose-response, caution scaling with balances, ties the governability to the debt rather than to disposition, and the fade after payoff completes the isolation, the discipline lifting when the obligation does.
explanations.C: This choice addresses how loans are entered, not what they do afterward. Freely chosen debt can discipline as thoroughly as imposed debt; the claim concerns the leverage the balance creates, whatever the signature's willingness.
explanations.D: This choice counts meetings, a proxy for creditor attention rather than debtor compliance; delegations visiting says nothing about whether the indebted state accepts terms it would otherwise refuse, which is the governability at issue.
